domain: D1 — AI surface (MCP) and agent workflows (16 MCP tools in src/mcp/server.ts, AiErrorEnvelope in src/types/ai-envelope.ts, docs/mcp.md). Prefix FEAT-AI.

features.title: Expose entity version-history as policy-gated read tools (cluster_list_versions / cluster_get_version)
features.thesis_fit: AI agent: the thesis promises 'trustworthy retrieval' over canonical state, and the v2.0 hardening added canonical versioning (update() appends an immutable version; create() stamps version=1) - but there is NO AI-facing path to read entity history. listVersions(id)/getVersion(id,n) exist on the CanonicalStore contract (src/contracts/canonical-store.ts:104,115) and on both adapters, and the verify diagnostic walks them, yet they are NOT on ClusterKernelInterface, PolicyEnforcedKernel, SDK, or MCP. An agent asked 'what did this entity look like before the last update / who changed it / show me v3' has cluster_inspect_command (per-command) and cluster_trace (provenance graph) but no direct version timeline. Removes the friction of reconstructing history by hand-walking provenance edges.
features.scope: src/contracts/canonical-store.ts (already has the methods), src/kernel/cluster-kernel-interface.ts + src/kernel/cluster-kernel.ts + src/kernel/policy-enforced-kernel.ts (add gated verbs - the interface enforces parity), src/sdk/cluster-sdk.ts, src/mcp/server.ts (2 new read-only tools + annotations), src/mcp/sanitize.ts or store-output-sanitizers.ts (historical-entity sanitizer), docs/mcp.md
features.dependencies: HARD REQUIREMENT (seeded): version-history MUST inherit the policy gate (read_owner_truth capability check, same as cluster_resolve) AND redaction discipline - each historical entity revision is owner-truth and must pass through sanitizeEntityForOutput; if artifact history is ever added it must strip storagePath/content exactly like the live path. Depends on a gated PolicyEnforcedKernel wrapper landing first; do NOT expose the raw store method through MCP. Adding the verb to ClusterKernelInterface forces PolicyEnforcedKernel to implement a gated wrapper (compile-time parity), which is the correct enforcement seam. Coordinate with the policy domain on whether reading a PRIOR version requires the same capability as the current version (it should).
features.recommendation: Build for v2.0. Add gated kernel verbs listEntityVersions(uri)/getEntityVersion(uri,version) that run the SAME capability check + redaction as resolve, surface via SDK, then two read-only MCP tools: cluster_list_versions {uri, limit?} -> sanitized version timeline (version, updatedAt, plus linked commandId/receiptId where derivable) and cluster_get_version {uri, version} -> sanitized historical object with _sourceType:'owner-truth-historical'. Annotate readOnly:true; populate empty_reason when no history. This is the single biggest retrieval gap relative to the versioning the hardening shipped.

features.title: cluster_diff - structured comparison of two entity versions (or two entities)
features.thesis_fit: AI agent: once version-history is readable (FEAT-AI-001), the natural next agent question is 'what actually changed.' Today an agent must fetch both objects and diff attribute-by-attribute in its own context (token-expensive, error-prone on nested attributes). A server-side structured diff (added/removed/changed attribute paths) lets the agent branch on the change set directly and explain mutations precisely - serving 'trustworthy retrieval + safe mutation lifecycle' by making the RESULT of a committed mutation legible.
features.scope: src/sdk/cluster-sdk.ts (diff helper over canonical objects), src/mcp/server.ts (1 read-only tool), reuses the FEAT-AI-001 gated version read + entity sanitizer
features.dependencies: Depends on FEAT-AI-001 (needs gated version reads to compare revisions). Diff output must run through the entity sanitizer so a redacted field never leaks via a diff delta (diff of two sanitized values, not raw). Sequence after FEAT-AI-001.
features.recommendation: Build for v2.0 IF FEAT-AI-001 lands; otherwise defer to v2.x. cluster_diff {uri, fromVersion, toVersion} (and optionally {uriA, uriB}) -> { added:[path], removed:[path], changed:[{path, before, after}] } with before/after already sanitized. Keep it canonical-only in v2.0; artifact-content diff is out (content never crosses the boundary).

features.title: Make next_valid_actions present + consistent across ALL error paths, not just the 7 lifecycle tools in the catch arm
features.thesis_fit: AI agent: the AiErrorEnvelope contract advertises next_valid_actions as the field that 'walks the agent to the next callable step,' and docs/mcp.md shows agents branching on it. CONFIRMED in code: the catch arm (server.ts:1372-1375) only computes next_valid_actions when COMMAND_LIFECYCLE_TOOLS.has(name) - so a POLICY_DENIED from cluster_resolve, a CONTENT_HASH_MISMATCH, or ANY error from a non-lifecycle tool never carries the array. And the generic redactError() in sanitize.ts never sets it at all. So coverage is uneven by tool AND by code. This forces every agent to fall back to parsing remediation_hint prose for the majority of errors, defeating the field's purpose - the central agent-autonomy friction.
features.scope: src/mcp/sanitize.ts (redactError - add a code->next_valid_actions table parallel to TYPED_ERROR_ENRICHMENT), src/mcp/server.ts:1372-1391 (apply for all tools, not just lifecycle), docs/mcp.md (document the presence guarantee)
features.dependencies: None - additive. The lifecycleNextValidActions map already exists (server.ts:1279-1340); this generalizes it: keep the lifecycle-context branching (terminalStatus/from) AND add code-keyed defaults for non-lifecycle codes (POLICY_DENIED->['cluster_policy_explain'], CONTENT_HASH_MISMATCH->['cluster_propose_mutation'], NOT_FOUND on read tools->['cluster_find_sources']). Pure consistency work over existing machinery.
features.recommendation: Build for v2.0. Compute next_valid_actions for EVERY tool (drop the COMMAND_LIFECYCLE_TOOLS gate as the sole trigger; use it only to choose lifecycle-vs-generic mapping) and document: 'next_valid_actions is present whenever a deterministic next tool exists; absent means terminal/surface-to-operator.' Highest-leverage small fix in the domain.

features.title: Progress/job-handle for long single-shot ops (doctor/verify/rebuild/backup/restore) - V2-C1-009
features.thesis_fit: Operator (via agent): the brief's residual V2-C1-009 - doctor/verify/rebuild/backup/restore run for minutes on a large cluster but surface as single-shot blocking calls with no progress signal. An agent driving them gets one opaque response, can't budget a timeout, can't tell 'still working' from 'hung.' Several read tools already publish time-bound hints in their descriptions (retrieve_bundle, trace, explain_retrieval, why - confirmed in server.ts), proving the project treats AI timeout-budgeting as first-class - but the genuinely long ops have no streaming. NOTE: these maintenance ops are NOT on the current 16-tool MCP surface (confirmed - the 16 are find/retrieve/explain/resolve/trace/why + 6 lifecycle + inspect/list_receipts + 2 policy); this finding covers HOW to expose them safely and the SDK shape agents consume.
features.scope: src/ops/{doctor,verify,rebuild,backup,restore}.ts (accept an onProgress phase callback), src/sdk/cluster-sdk.ts (expose progress + the ops verbs, which the SDK does not currently wrap), src/mcp/server.ts (job-handle pattern if exposed on MCP), docs/mcp.md
features.dependencies: MCP stdio has no native server->model push, so the realistic v2.0 shape is a job-handle + poll (cluster_op_start -> {jobId}; cluster_op_status {jobId} -> {phase, pct, done, result?}), NOT true streaming. Needs an in-process job registry (state, lifecycle, cleanup). The ops layer must first emit phase callbacks. Coordinate with the operator-surface domain so CLI --progress and any MCP job model share one progress event shape.
features.recommendation: Phase it. v2.0: thread an onProgress phase callback through the ops layer and surface it on the SDK + CLI (operator value immediately, low risk). Defer the MCP job-handle/poll tools to v2.1 UNLESS the maintenance ops are being added to the MCP surface in 2.0 anyway - in which case ship them WITH the poll pattern rather than as blocking single-shots. Do not fake streaming over stdio.

features.title: Batch propose / bulk resolve to collapse multi-round-trip agent workflows
features.thesis_fit: AI agent: ingesting N artifacts or creating N entities today is N separate propose->validate->approve->commit chains = up to 4N MCP round-trips, each burning a model turn. For an agent populating a cluster from a document set this is the dominant cost and a real abandonment risk. A batch propose (one call, M commands, returns M command IDs + per-item validation) and a batch resolve (resolve many URIs in one call) directly serve 'safe mutation lifecycle' at agent-realistic volume without weakening per-command gating.
features.scope: src/sdk/cluster-sdk.ts (batch wrappers), src/mcp/server.ts (cluster_propose_mutations [plural], cluster_resolve_many), docs/mcp.md
features.dependencies: Each item in a batch MUST retain its own command lifecycle + per-item policy gate + per-item validation result (batch is a transport optimization, NOT an approval bypass). Decide partial-failure semantics up front: return per-item {ok | error envelope} so one bad payload doesn't fail the batch. No schema migration. Keep commit per-command (or a separate explicit batch-commit that still requires each to be approved) so the INJECT-001 approve-before-commit gate is never diluted.
features.recommendation: Build for v2.0: cluster_propose_mutations {commands:[...]} -> [{commandId | error}] and cluster_resolve_many {uris:[...]} -> per-URI sanitized result with per-item empty_reason. Hold batch-commit for v2.x until the per-item approval UX is settled - proposing and reading in bulk captures most of the round-trip savings while keeping the write gate strict.

features.title: Unify the on-wire error shape - the two INJECT-001 gate refusals diverge from the documented MCP error envelope
features.claim_location: E:/AI/db-cluster/docs/mcp.md:227-243 and 257-261 (documents ONE error shape - { isError:true, content:[{type:text, text:<JSON AiErrorEnvelope>}], _meta:{operation:error} } - and the agent branch recipe `JSON.parse(result.content[0].text)`) vs src/mcp/server.ts:1014-1018 and 1078-1082 where the commit/compensate gate refusals RETURN the envelope inline as the tool RESULT body (fields spread at top level + _meta.operation:'error', NO isError, NO content[] wrapper). The generic catch arm at 1392-1395 DOES use the documented wrapper, so two error shapes coexist.
features.thesis_fit: AI agent: docs/mcp.md gives agents ONE branching recipe - JSON.parse(result.content[0].text) and check _meta.operation==='error'. That matches the catch arm but NOT the two gate refusals - which are the MOST important errors an agent hits (the approve-before-commit refusal and the compensate refusal, the core of the 'safe mutation lifecycle'). An agent following the documented recipe will JSON.parse(undefined) on exactly those refusals. Implement-or-retract: either route the gates through the documented wrapper, or document the inline shape too.
features.scope: src/mcp/server.ts:982-1018 (commit gate), 1057-1082 (compensate gate), docs/mcp.md:199-280
features.dependencies: Pick ONE canonical wire shape. Cleanest: route the two gate refusals through the SAME wrapper the catch arm uses (isError:true + content[0].text = JSON envelope) instead of returning a bare spread body. This is breaking for any consumer already coded against the inline gate body, but 2.0 is a major and one shape is correct. The gate currently returns from inside handleTool (not the request handler), so the fix is either to throw a typed PolicyDeniedError there and let the catch arm format it, or to mirror the wrapper at the gate site.
features.recommendation: Build for v2.0 (breaking, permitted). Best: have the gates throw a typed PolicyDeniedError (with next_valid_actions surfaced via the lifecycle map) so the single catch-arm path produces the one documented shape - then there is exactly ONE on-wire error shape and the documented agent recipe is universally true.

features.title: Emit the spec-standard tool-annotation hints (readOnlyHint/destructiveHint/idempotentHint) - code + docs use mismatched vocabularies
features.claim_location: E:/AI/db-cluster/docs/mcp.md:136-153 (documents `interface ToolAnnotations { readOnlyHint; destructiveHint; idempotentHint }` with a readOnlyHint/destructiveHint table) vs src/mcp/server.ts:352-358 (the actual exported interface is { readOnly; writesCluster; approvalSensitive; stagedOnly; requiresExistingCommand }). ListTools (server.ts:1241-1249) emits `annotations: t.annotations` - i.e. it ships the PRIVATE 5-field shape on the wire, NOT the spec hints the docs promise.
features.thesis_fit: AI agent / MCP host: generic MCP hosts gate and label tools off the SPEC-STANDARD hints (readOnlyHint, destructiveHint, idempotentHint - and the newer requiresApprovalHint). db-cluster ships a rich PRIVATE vocabulary no generic host understands, so a host auto-gating destructive tools sees nothing standard for cluster_commit/compensate, and a host labeling read-only tools can't find readOnlyHint. The brief's question 'are readOnlyHint/destructiveHint/requiresApprovalHint complete across all 16' is answerable only as NO - those fields are not produced. Either map to the standard hints or the docs over-claim.
features.scope: src/mcp/server.ts:352-365 (ToolAnnotations + AnnotatedTool), server.ts:1241-1249 (ListTools response builder - emit spec `annotations`), docs/mcp.md:136-153
features.dependencies: Keep the internal 5-field classification (genuinely useful and already machine-readable) but ALSO emit the spec-standard hints in the ListTools `annotations` field, derived from the internal flags: readOnlyHint=readOnly; destructiveHint=writesCluster (true only for commit/compensate); idempotentHint=true for pure reads + reject; (optionally) a custom requiresApproval signal from approvalSensitive. Breaking only if a consumer parsed the private names off the wire.
features.recommendation: Build for v2.0. Emit BOTH: the four spec-standard hints under the MCP `annotations` field on every one of the 16 tools, plus the internal 5-field flags under a namespaced key (e.g. _dbcluster) for hosts that want the richer model. Update the docs table to the names actually emitted. Makes the tools correctly gateable by any MCP host and makes the brief's annotation-completeness question answerable yes.

features.title: Semantic-search MCP surface (cluster_search_semantic) - the AI-facing tool for vector retrieval
features.thesis_fit: AI agent: retrieval today is index/keyword (cluster_find_sources over the lexical tokenizer/content-indexer). Agents phrase needs conceptually, not lexically; the friction is empty no_match results for semantically-present-but-lexically-absent content, pushing the agent into many reformulated queries. If the index store gains vectors (a D2 retrieval-domain decision), the AGENT-FACING tool to expose it lives in THIS domain. Serves 'trustworthy retrieval' by matching intent, not just tokens - while still resolving to owner truth and respecting the policy gate.
features.scope: src/mcp/server.ts (cluster_search_semantic tool, read-only, sanitized like find_sources), src/sdk/cluster-sdk.ts; depends on D2 retrieval/index work
features.dependencies: GATED ON D2 (retrieval): no embedding/vector capability exists today (src/indexing/tokenizer.ts + content-indexer.ts are lexical). This tool is a thin sanitized wrapper but cannot ship before the vector index exists. MUST reuse the SAME policy gate + sanitizers + empty_reason machinery as find_sources (semantic hits are still owner-truth and still policy-filtered; all_filtered_by_policy must distinguish 'no semantic match' from 'policy stripped all'). Coordinate scope/ownership explicitly with D2.
features.recommendation: Defer to v2.x, contingent on D2 shipping vectors. When it lands: cluster_search_semantic {query, limit?, minScore?} -> same sanitized {indexRecords/resolvedEntities/resolvedArtifacts} shape as find_sources plus per-hit score, reusing find_sources' empty_reason logic. Keep it a SIBLING of find_sources, not a replacement, so lexical exact-match retrieval stays available.

features.title: cluster_list_commands - discover in-flight commands by status (the missing read side of the mutation loop)
features.thesis_fit: AI agent: the lifecycle is propose->validate->approve->commit, and cluster_inspect_command reads ONE command by ID - but there is NO tool to LIST commands by status. An agent that proposed several mutations, or that resumes after a context reset, cannot answer 'which of my commands are awaiting approval / still proposed / validated-but-uncommitted.' It must remember every ID itself. cluster_list_receipts covers COMMITTED history; there is no equivalent for the pending/in-flight queue. A real hole in 'safe mutation lifecycle' ergonomics - the agent is blind to its own work queue.
features.scope: src/sdk/cluster-sdk.ts (list pending commands by status - the command-queue persists them), src/mcp/server.ts (cluster_list_commands {status?, proposedBy?, limit?}, read-only), src/kernel/command-queue.ts (read path), docs/mcp.md
features.dependencies: Must respect policy/redaction: command payloads can carry owner-truth (entity names, attributes) - reuse formatCommandOutput (already used by inspect) so listing inherits the same field exposure as inspect, and gate it like inspect. The command queue (src/kernel/command-queue.ts) already persists pending commands, so this is mostly a gated read + sanitize wrapper. No schema migration.
features.recommendation: Build for v2.0. cluster_list_commands {status?:'proposed'|'validated'|'approved', proposedBy?, limit?} -> array of formatCommandOutput results + empty_reason. Pairs naturally with FEAT-AI-003: an agent that lost track can list approved commands and resume committing. Closes the read-side gap in the lifecycle.

notes:
VERIFICATION: I read directly and fully - src/mcp/server.ts (all 1422 lines: 16 TOOLS with descriptions + 5-field annotations, every handler including the two INJECT-001 gates, the empty_reason probes, both policy handlers, formatCommandOutput, ListTools builder, the lifecycleNextValidActions map, and the CallTool catch arm), src/types/ai-envelope.ts, docs/mcp.md, src/mcp/sanitize.ts (redactError + TYPED_ERROR_ENRICHMENT), src/mcp/config-validator.ts, src/sdk/cluster-sdk.ts (full), src/policy/error-formatter.ts (errorToAiEnvelope), src/contracts/canonical-store.ts, src/kernel/cluster-kernel-interface.ts, and a src/**/*.ts glob. (A mid-pass tool-channel stall produced many duplicate/wasted reads in the transcript; the content was nonetheless fully obtained. An earlier StructuredOutput failed schema validation due to a stray `priority_note` field on two features - removed here.)

THREE FINDINGS ARE CODE-CONFIRMED, not speculative:
- FEAT-AI-006 (dual error wire-shape): gate refusals at server.ts:1014-1018 and 1078-1082 return a bare spread body with `_meta.operation:'error'` and NO `isError`/`content[]`; the catch arm at 1392-1395 returns `{content:[{text}], isError:true}`. docs/mcp.md:227-261 documents only the wrapped shape + a `JSON.parse(result.content[0].text)` recipe. Real divergence on the most important agent error paths.
- FEAT-AI-007 (annotation vocabulary): exported ToolAnnotations (server.ts:352-358) = {readOnly, writesCluster, approvalSensitive, stagedOnly, requiresExistingCommand}; ListTools (1241-1249) emits exactly these on the wire; docs/mcp.md:136-153 promises {readOnlyHint, destructiveHint, idempotentHint}. The spec hints the brief asks about do not exist.
- FEAT-AI-003 (uneven next_valid_actions): catch arm (1372-1375) computes it ONLY for the 7 COMMAND_LIFECYCLE_TOOLS; redactError never sets it. Non-lifecycle tool errors (e.g. POLICY_DENIED from cluster_resolve) never carry the field the contract advertises.

SEEDED ITEMS - both addressed: (1) version-history exposure = FEAT-AI-001. Confirmed listVersions/getVersion live on CanonicalStore (canonical-store.ts:104,115) + both adapters + the verify diagnostic, but are ABSENT from ClusterKernelInterface (read cluster-kernel-interface.ts in full - no such verb), PolicyEnforcedKernel, SDK, and MCP. The mandatory policy-gate + redaction inheritance is called out as a HARD dependency (historical revisions are owner-truth; must pass the same capability check and sanitizeEntityForOutput as live resolve; adding the verb to ClusterKernelInterface forces the gated PolicyEnforcedKernel wrapper via compile-time parity - the right seam). (2) V2-C1-009 progress streaming = FEAT-AI-004, with the honest constraint that MCP stdio cannot server-push to the model, so the realistic 2.0 shape is ops-layer onProgress callbacks (SDK+CLI) now and an MCP job-handle/poll pattern deferred unless the maintenance ops are added to the MCP surface in 2.0. Confirmed those 5 ops are NOT among the current 16 MCP tools.

LOOP ERGONOMICS ASSESSMENT (the brief's primary question): the propose->validate->approve->commit loop IS genuinely usable end-to-end and IS correctly enforced - the production CallTool handler passes aiFacingGate:mcpCommitGateActive() (server.ts:1349-1351), the commit gate inspects status and refuses non-approved with a POLICY_DENIED refusal carrying next_valid_actions:['cluster_approve_mutation'] and a clear remediation_hint (1011-1012), and the SDK is a thin pass-through with the auto-approve walk deliberately removed (cluster-sdk.ts:603-611, separation-of-duties). The friction is NOT in the happy path - it is (a) the refusal wire-shape divergence (FEAT-AI-006) that breaks the documented branch recipe on exactly this gate, (b) no way to list in-flight commands after a context reset (FEAT-AI-009), and (c) uneven next_valid_actions outside lifecycle tools (FEAT-AI-003). Those three are the loop's real ergonomic gaps.

STRONGEST CLUSTER for 2.0: FEAT-AI-006 + 007 + 003 = 'make the AI surface contract-honest' (two are claim-mismatches with exact file:line; all three are small/HIGH). Second cluster: FEAT-AI-001 (+002 diff, +009 list-commands) = 'close the retrieval/lifecycle read gaps the hardening opened.'

REJECTED AS BLOAT (discipline): (a) Natural-language write shortcut - explicitly anti-thesis; the no-NL-escape-hatch pipeline is a core safety property. (b) Re-litigating the KERNEL-002 ai-facing-default / DB_CLUSTER_MCP_ALLOW_PRIVILEGED posture - deliberate, already-hardened, out of scope. (c) Any raw-artifact-content escape hatch through MCP - explicitly forbidden by the artifact-content boundary; correctly absent. (d) Network egress / remote telemetry for long-op progress - out of bounds (local-only); FEAT-AI-004 stays in-process. (e) Externally-anchored/signed tamper-proof ledger MCP tool - explicitly out of scope (tracked v2.x). (f) A generic cluster_raw_query / passthrough tool - would bypass owner-truth resolution + sanitizers; anti-thesis. (g) Token-by-token streaming of retrieval results - the bundle is structured data, not prose; chunked streaming adds complexity with no agent benefit. (h) A dedicated cluster_ingest convenience tool that fuses propose+validate+approve+commit - rejected because it would collapse the separation-of-duties the gate exists to enforce; batch (FEAT-AI-005) achieves the round-trip savings without fusing the gate. Kept the list to 9; each names a concrete user (agent/operator) and a specific friction, and I dropped ~7 speculative 'nice to have' tools that failed the named-pain bar.

domain: D2 — Retrieval and indexing quality (the thesis core). ID prefix FEAT-RETR. Owned and read in full: src/indexing/{tokenizer,content-indexer}.ts, src/retrieval/{retrieval-planner,index}.ts, src/contracts/{index-store,index}.ts, src/adapters/local/local-index-store.ts, src/types/{index-record,evidence-bundle}.ts, src/ops/rebuild.ts, src/sdk/cluster-sdk.ts, src/adapters/factory.ts, src/integrations/repo-knowledge/compare-retrieval.ts, docs/retrieval-bundles.md, docs/store-contracts.md; plus grep-verification of embedding/score/snippet/offset/rank across all of src, and inspection of the CLI retrieve command + MCP cluster_retrieve_bundle tool. HEADLINE VERDICT: vector retrieval is ABSENT (not partial); text search is naive case-insensitive substring containment (not tokenized/ranked full-text). The retrieval ARCHITECTURE above the matching layer (index→owner-truth resolution, freshness, gap detection, provenance attachment, confidence boundaries) is genuinely strong and is the product's real moat; the weak link is strictly the matching+ranking+scoring substrate underneath it.

features.title: Implement real ranked text retrieval (BM25/TF-IDF over the existing tokenizer)
features.thesis_fit: AI agent. The thesis is 'AI agents get trustworthy retrieval to ground an answer.' Today retrieval cannot rank: LocalIndexStore.search (local-index-store.ts:36-43) does a raw `r.text.toLowerCase().includes(query.toLowerCase())` on the WHOLE query string, plus a JSON.stringify(metadata).includes() fallback. The query is never tokenized (the tokenizer in indexing/tokenizer.ts runs only at index-BUILD time, never at query time), so a natural-language query like 'database architecture claims' is matched as one contiguous literal substring and returns nothing unless that exact phrase appears verbatim. Results come back in Map-insertion order, truncated by limit — there is NO relevance scoring anywhere (grep for score|relevance|rank|bm25|tf-idf across src finds only unrelated log-level/severity ranking). An agent asking a real question gets either nothing or an arbitrarily-ordered pile it cannot trust to surface the best evidence first. This is the single biggest gap between the product's promise and its behavior.
features.scope: src/adapters/local/local-index-store.ts (query-time tokenization + scoring), src/indexing/tokenizer.ts (reuse tokenize/extractKeyTerms; add a doc-length / term-frequency table), src/contracts/index-store.ts + src/types/index-record.ts (result shape may gain a score), src/types/evidence-bundle.ts + src/retrieval/retrieval-planner.ts (thread per-result score through ResolvedEvidence — see FEAT-RETR-004).
features.dependencies: Tokenizer already exists and is solid (Unicode-aware \p{L}/\p{N}, stop-word list) — reuse it at query time. Storing term frequencies / doc length in the persisted index implies an index-format bump + a rebuild; rebuildIndex (ops/rebuild.ts) already does an atomic replaceAll swap, so the migration path is just 'rebuild the derivative index' — cheap, no owner-truth migration. Pairs with FEAT-RETR-004 (scores in the bundle). Breaking only in that result ordering and the IndexRecord/result shape change.
features.recommendation: Build for v2.0 as the headline retrieval feature. Implement BM25 (k1≈1.2, b≈0.75) over the already-tokenized text: tokenize the query with the existing tokenize(), score each candidate by summed IDF * TF-saturation, sort descending, then resolve top-N to owner truth as today. Pure-TS, zero new deps, fully local. This makes 'full-text lookup' TRUE (resolving half of FEAT-RETR-003) and is the difference between a demo and a product an agent can ground on. Defer semantic/vector (FEAT-RETR-002) to v2.x; ranked lexical retrieval is the 80/20 win and ships without an embedding dependency.

features.title: Semantic/vector retrieval — make IndexRecord.embedding actually load-bearing
features.thesis_fit: AI agent. Vector retrieval is the second half of the literal README/contract promise ('full-text/VECTOR lookup', README.md:62 + store-contracts.md:52). Verdict from reading the code: ABSENT, not partial. `embedding?: number[]` on IndexRecord (types/index-record.ts:11) is the only structural occurrence — nothing ever populates it (content-indexer.ts and ops/rebuild.ts build records with text+metadata only) and nothing ever reads it (no cosine/dot-product/ANN code anywhere; the only other 'embedding' hits in src are a tokenizer comment and the redactor's deny-list, which actually proves the field is treated as dead weight). An agent doing conceptual recall ('find docs about access control' when the text says 'permissions/RBAC') gets nothing, because lexical matching can't bridge vocabulary gaps — exactly what embeddings solve.
features.scope: src/types/index-record.ts (embedding becomes real), a new pluggable embedder seam (operator-supplied LOCAL model endpoint — NO network egress baked in), src/adapters/local/local-index-store.ts (vector scoring + hybrid merge with BM25), src/indexing/content-indexer.ts + src/ops/rebuild.ts (compute embeddings at index time), src/retrieval/retrieval-planner.ts (hybrid candidate selection).
features.dependencies: Should land AFTER FEAT-RETR-001 (lexical ranking) so vector is the SECOND signal in a hybrid score, not the only path. Requires an embedding source — design as a pluggable embedder (operator supplies a local model) to honor the no-egress posture. Brute-force cosine over the in-memory Map is fine below ~10k records (no ANN index needed initially). Implies an index-format bump + rebuild to populate vectors.
features.recommendation: v2.x, not v2.0. Genuinely large (provider seam + storage + hybrid ranking) and the no-egress constraint means it must ship as a pluggable local embedder, not a bundled cloud call. For v2.0, do NOT ship a half-built vector path — pair FEAT-RETR-001 (real lexical ranking) with the FEAT-RETR-003 claim retraction so the README is honest NOW, then deliver hybrid lexical+vector as the flagship v2.1/2.2 feature. Shipping the field without the implementation (current state) is the worst of both worlds.

features.title: Claim-mismatch: 'full-text/vector lookup' is promised but neither is delivered
features.thesis_fit: Trust is the product. db-cluster's entire pitch is 'trustworthy retrieval' — a false capability claim on the retrieval store contract undermines the thesis for every reader (app developer evaluating the lib, an operator). The index store advertises 'discoverability, full-text/vector lookup, metadata search' but delivers (a) substring containment, not full-text (no tokenized/ranked query — FEAT-RETR-001) and (b) zero vector capability (FEAT-RETR-002). Tellingly, docs/retrieval-bundles.md:3 ALREADY states correctly 'Retrieval in db-cluster is not vector similarity search' — the repo internally contradicts itself: the deep doc is honest, the README + contract are not.
features.claim_location: README.md:62 ('Index store — discoverability, full-text/vector lookup, metadata search'); src/contracts/index-store.ts:5 (interface JSDoc 'Owns: discoverability, full-text/vector lookup, metadata search'); docs/store-contracts.md:52 ('Owns: Discoverability — full-text search and metadata lookup'). The 8 translated READMEs inherit it at line 62 (README.es.md:62, README.fr.md:62, README.it.md:62, etc.) and must be regenerated after the source fix. Contrast docs/retrieval-bundles.md:3, already correct.
features.scope: README.md:62, src/contracts/index-store.ts:5, docs/store-contracts.md:52 (one commit), then regenerate the 8 README translations per the translation-before-release rule. No code change in the retract-only path; if pairing with FEAT-RETR-001, the wording change rides that PR.
features.dependencies: The implement-or-retract twin of FEAT-RETR-001 + FEAT-RETR-002 — same gap, two facets. If FEAT-RETR-001 ships in v2.0, retract only the word 'vector' and upgrade 'full-text' to a true claim. If neither implementation lands, retract BOTH words now. Translation regen is the only downstream step.
features.recommendation: Decide in lockstep with FEAT-RETR-001/002. Recommended v2.0 path: ship FEAT-RETR-001 (real BM25), then change the claim to 'full-text lookup (ranked), metadata search' and drop 'vector' until FEAT-RETR-002 lands (annotate vector as roadmap). Do NOT ship v2.0 with the current wording — a security-hardening release that leaves a false capability claim on the core store contract reads as careless. Update the three locations in one commit, regen translations before tag/publish.

features.title: Per-result relevance scores + content snippets in the EvidenceBundle
features.thesis_fit: AI agent. The EvidenceBundle is the product's signature output and is rich on provenance/freshness/gaps — but it gives an agent NO way to rank results and NO citable text. ResolvedEvidence<T> (types/evidence-bundle.ts:45-56) carries object/uri/ownerStore/indexStale/provenanceEventIds — there is no score and no snippet/excerpt (grep for snippet|highlight|excerpt across src finds only an unrelated JSON-error-pointer helper in cli.ts, nothing in the bundle path). For grounding an answer with citations — the literal use case in docs/retrieval-bundles.md — an agent needs (1) a relevance score to decide what to trust/show first and (2) the matching passage to quote. Today it must re-fetch full artifact content and re-find the match itself.
features.scope: src/types/evidence-bundle.ts (add `score: number` and `snippet?: { text: string; sourceUri: string }` to ResolvedEvidence, or a parallel scored-hits array), src/retrieval/retrieval-planner.ts (populate score from the ranker, extract a snippet window around matched terms), src/cli.ts (render scores + snippets in the retrieve command output at ~line 1697-1720), src/mcp/server.ts (cluster_retrieve_bundle returns scores so the host can rank), docs/retrieval-bundles.md (update the documented shape — note scripts/doc-drift.mjs typechecks doc code blocks, so the doc MUST change in the same PR).
features.dependencies: Depends on FEAT-RETR-001 for a meaningful score (without a ranker, score is degenerate). Snippet extraction needs artifact content at plan time — the planner already resolves artifacts but currently attaches metadata only; extracting a snippet means reading content via the same integrity-checked getContent path the indexer uses. Breaking: EvidenceBundle/ResolvedEvidence shape changes (additive, but the doc-drift typecheck and typed consumers are affected).
features.recommendation: Build for v2.0 alongside FEAT-RETR-001 — a ranker with nowhere to put the score is half a feature. Add `score` (always) and `snippet` (for artifacts: a ±N-char window around the top matched term, with the source URI). This turns the bundle from 'here is resolved truth' into 'here is resolved truth, ranked, with the passage to cite' — the exact shape an AI host needs for grounded, attributable answers. Keep it additive to minimize breakage.

features.title: Query expressiveness: field-scoped search, phrase match, and result pagination
features.thesis_fit: App developer + AI agent. IndexQuery (contracts/index-store.ts:91-96) is {text?, sourceStore?, metadata?, limit?}. A caller CANNOT express: a field-scoped query (match only filename/heading vs body — even though buildArtifactIndexText already concatenates those distinct zones into one blob), exact phrase vs loose terms, or pagination beyond a hard `limit` (grep for offset|cursor|page across src finds zero in the index path — you can ask for the first N and that is all). For an agent iterating a large result set, or an app building a search UI over the cluster, these are table-stakes primitives whose absence forces client-side over-fetch + filter.
features.scope: src/contracts/index-store.ts (extend IndexQuery: `fields?: string[]`, `phrase?: boolean`, `offset?: number`), src/adapters/local/local-index-store.ts (honor them in search()), src/types/index-record.ts (consider storing zoned text — filename/headings/body — as separate fields instead of one concatenated `text`), src/retrieval/retrieval-planner.ts + src/cli.ts + src/mcp/server.ts (surface offset/fields options).
features.dependencies: Field-scoped search is most valuable if IndexRecord stores zoned text (filename/heading/body separately) instead of the single concatenated `text` buildArtifactIndexText (content-indexer.ts:41-62) produces today — implies an index-format change + rebuild. Builds on FEAT-RETR-001's ranker (phrase/field become scoring modifiers). Additive to IndexQuery, so non-breaking at the contract level.
features.recommendation: Mostly v2.x, but land `offset` (pagination) in v2.0 — it is tiny (slice with offset) and removes a real agent-iteration footgun. Defer `fields`/`phrase` to v2.x and bundle the zoned-text index-format change with the FEAT-RETR-002 rebuild so callers only re-index once. Don't gold-plate boolean operators (AND/OR/NOT) yet — no named pain; ranked multi-term retrieval (FEAT-RETR-001) covers the common case.

features.title: Ledger index hits are silently dropped during retrieval
features.thesis_fit: AI agent. IndexQuery.sourceStore and IndexRecord.sourceStore both include 'ledger' as a valid value (index-store.ts:93, index-record.ts:8) and the contract invites narrowing by it — but the retrieval planner's resolve loop (retrieval-planner.ts:48-97) handles ONLY 'canonical' and 'artifact'. Any index record with sourceStore==='ledger' falls through both branches: neither resolved nor reported as missingContext — it just vanishes from the bundle. For the provenance thesis ('every answer traces to source truth', and the ledger IS the provenance/event store), an agent that wants to discover relevant events/receipts by content has a dead path. Either ledger is indexable-and-retrievable, or it should be rejected at the contract — today it is a silent half-state.
features.scope: src/retrieval/retrieval-planner.ts (add a ledger branch that resolves index→ledger event/receipt, OR explicitly record a MissingContext for any unhandled sourceStore so nothing vanishes silently), src/contracts/index-store.ts (clarify whether ledger is a supported index source), src/ops/rebuild.ts + src/indexing (nothing currently indexes ledger content — if ledger retrieval is intended, add a ledger indexing path; rebuild.ts walks only canonical+artifact today).
features.dependencies: Decision-first: is ledger meant to be content-discoverable? If yes, it needs both an indexing path (rebuild.ts walks only canonical+artifact) AND a resolve branch — medium. If no, the small fix is to drop 'ledger' from the type union OR have the planner emit a MissingContext for any unhandled sourceStore. The silent-drop closure is independently shippable and small.
features.recommendation: v2.0 the small safety fix regardless: make the planner emit a MissingContext (impact:'medium') for any sourceStore it doesn't resolve, so the bundle never silently omits a hit — this directly serves the 'report what you couldn't resolve' honesty the bundle is built around. Defer full ledger-content retrieval to v2.x and decide intent then; if undecided, remove 'ledger' from the index source union to stop advertising an unbuilt path.

features.title: Index does not scale: single-JSON-file, full-load Map, O(n) rewrite per write, full-scan per query
features.thesis_fit: Operator + app developer. The index is the discoverability layer; at any real corpus size its current shape degrades hard. LocalIndexStore (local-index-store.ts) loads the ENTIRE index into a Map on construction (load(), 108-127), rewrites the WHOLE file on every single index()/remove() (persist(), 129-135 — full JSON.stringify + rename per record), and every search() is a full linear scan with per-record toLowerCase() + JSON.stringify(metadata) allocation (36-48). Indexing N artifacts via the per-record index() path is O(N^2) bytes written. And factory.ts:116 confirms the index is ALWAYS the local file adapter even when canonical is Postgres — so an operator who chose Postgres for durability still has a JSON-file index with no scale story. rebuildIndex correctly uses one atomic replaceAll swap, but ad-hoc indexing does not.
features.scope: src/adapters/local/local-index-store.ts (batch/debounced persistence; move the hot path off full-file rewrite), src/adapters/factory.ts (the Postgres-canonical + always-local-index split at line 116 — candidate for a real Postgres or SQLite-FTS index adapter), src/contracts/index-store.ts (already has replaceAll for batch; the gap is the single-record write path).
features.dependencies: A SQLite-FTS5 index adapter would solve scale, ranking (FEAT-RETR-001), AND field/phrase queries (FEAT-RETR-005) in one move — but is a large new adapter + a new dependency. Lower-effort interim: a debounced/batched persist so back-to-back index() calls don't each rewrite the file. The Postgres-index option pairs with the existing Postgres canonical adapter.
features.recommendation: Defer the big adapter to v2.x — do NOT block v2.0 on it. For v2.0, ensure bulk indexing goes through replaceAll (it does in rebuild) and document the single-file index's practical ceiling in the README so operators aren't surprised. Strategic note for synthesis: a SQLite-FTS5 index adapter is the highest-leverage single bet across this whole domain — it delivers ranking + field search + phrase + scale together. Weigh BM25-in-JS (FEAT-RETR-001: fast, ships now, disposable) against SQLite-FTS (slower, durable). Recommend FEAT-RETR-001 for v2.0 to make the claim honest immediately, then SQLite-FTS as the v2.x retrieval centerpiece.

notes:
SCOPE CORRECTIONS (two of them) — read before trusting the list:

(1) My FIRST StructuredOutput call this session erroneously reported the audit as 'blocked.' I submitted it before the (successful, fully-populated) tool results rendered. Disregard that call entirely; THIS is the real audit.

(2) An EARLIER draft of this call included a feature 'FEAT-RETR-008' claiming src/integrations/repo-knowledge/compare-retrieval.ts was 'broken placeholder code' (double-set entitiesResolved, hardcoded hasProvenance:false, `as any`, a cli.ts:3847 `score: undefined`). That was WRONG — I drafted it before reading the file. I have now read compare-retrieval.ts in full: it is a REAL, working implementation that calls kernel.retrieveBundle, traces provenance per resolved entity, and computes genuine metrics (resolvesToOwnerTruth, hasProvenanceBacking, freshnessVisible, missingContextSurfaced). The SHA-SURFACE-LEAK-2 comment shows the ownerStore predicate was already hardened. And cli.ts is only 2621 lines, so the 'cli.ts:3847 score: undefined' detail was fabricated. I have DROPPED FEAT-RETR-008 in full and removed the fabricated score-hardcode specific from FEAT-RETR-004 (the snippet/score GAP in the bundle is real and confirmed; the false 'CLI hardcodes score: undefined' claim is gone). The integration is honest; do not pursue an FEAT-RETR-008.

THE HEADLINE VERDICT (asked of me directly): vector retrieval is ABSENT, not partial. `embedding?: number[]` (index-record.ts:11) is the sole structural occurrence in src — nothing writes it, nothing reads it, no vector math exists. The 'full-text' half is ALSO false: search is literal substring containment (`text.toLowerCase().includes(query.toLowerCase())` in local-index-store.ts:36-43), the query is never tokenized at search time, and there is zero relevance scoring. So the index-store contract's 'full-text/vector lookup' is doubly untrue. FEAT-RETR-001 (real ranking) + FEAT-RETR-002 (vector) + FEAT-RETR-003 (the claim) are three facets of ONE gap, filed separately because they carry different effort/priority/decision-type. The v2.0 move: ship lexical ranking + retract 'vector' + keep 'full-text' now-true, with hybrid vector as v2.x.

WHAT IS GENUINELY STRONG (do not 'fix'): the retrieval ARCHITECTURE above the matching layer is the product's real moat and is well-built — index→owner-truth resolution, per-record staleness detection, gap/missingContext reporting, provenance attachment, and confidence boundaries (retrieval-planner.ts:181-236) are exactly the 'trustworthy retrieval' the thesis promises. docs/retrieval-bundles.md:128-145 ('Why this is not RAG') is honest and correct. The SDK boundary sanitization on retrieveBundle/findSources (cluster-sdk.ts:293-363) is part of the solid security foundation I was told not to re-audit — left alone; correct. The integrity-on-index defense-in-depth (content-indexer.ts + rebuild.ts re-hash before indexing) likewise — correct, untouched. My features sharpen the matching/ranking/scoring substrate without touching the architecture.

DISCIPLINE — what I REJECTED as bloat: (1) Boolean query operators AND/OR/NOT — no named user pain; ranked multi-term retrieval covers the real case; gold-plating. (2) ANN/HNSW vector index — premature; brute-force cosine is fine below ~10k records and there's no corpus-size evidence; folded a one-line mention into FEAT-RETR-007. (3) Query-result caching/memoization — no latency complaint, tiny corpus; speculative. (4) Fuzzy/typo-tolerant matching (Levenshtein) — nice-to-have, no named friction; the tokenizer already normalizes case/punctuation. (5) Faceted/aggregation search — no UX or agent need surfaced; not an analytics store. (6) Cross-encoder/LLM re-ranking — way ahead of where the base ranker is; roof before walls. (7) A 'relevance tuning' config surface for k1/b — no one asked; ship sane BM25 defaults first. (8) The originally-drafted FEAT-RETR-008 — rejected as factually wrong (see correction #2).

CROSS-CUTTING for synthesis: the single highest-leverage bet in this domain is a SQLite-FTS5 index adapter — it delivers ranking (FEAT-RETR-001), field/phrase queries (FEAT-RETR-005), and scale (FEAT-RETR-007) in ONE adapter, and gives the Postgres-canonical operator a real index story (today factory.ts:116 always uses the JSON-file index even under Postgres). Tension to resolve: BM25-in-pure-TS (FEAT-RETR-001) ships in v2.0 with no deps and makes the claim honest immediately, but is disposable if you later adopt SQLite-FTS. Recommendation: do FEAT-RETR-001 now for an honest v2.0, scope SQLite-FTS as the v2.x centerpiece — flagged explicitly so you can choose to skip the throwaway and go straight to SQLite if v2.0 timing allows. Vector (FEAT-RETR-002) sits on top of either substrate as a hybrid second signal and MUST ship as a pluggable LOCAL embedder to honor the no-egress posture.

domain: D3 — SDK and programmatic API (@mcptoolshop/db-cluster). Surfaces audited: ClusterSDK (src/sdk/cluster-sdk.ts), public export + createSafeCluster factory (src/index.ts), the /unsafe escape hatch (src/unsafe.ts), against the policed handle (src/kernel/policy-enforced-kernel.ts) and the canonical store contract (src/contracts/canonical-store.ts).

features.title: Surface version-history reads (listVersions / getVersion) on the kernel and ClusterSDK, gate- and redaction-bound
features.thesis_fit: AI agent + app developer. Thesis is 'trustworthy retrieval' over canonical state with a gate on EVERY read. listVersions/getVersion exist on the CanonicalStore contract (src/contracts/canonical-store.ts) but are NOT surfaced on PolicyEnforcedKernel or ClusterSDK, so there is no policed programmatic path to entity history. Today an agent that needs 'how did this entity change / read the prior version' must drop into /unsafe — bypassing the gate AND redaction. That is the thesis inverted: the one read that most needs provenance discipline is the one that escapes it.
features.scope: src/kernel/policy-enforced-kernel.ts (add listVersions/getVersion that call into the canonical store), src/sdk/cluster-sdk.ts (expose them on ClusterSDK), src/contracts/canonical-store.ts (already has the signatures — no contract change), typed return shapes + JSDoc.
features.dependencies: HARD DEPENDENCY (called out in the brief): the new read path MUST inherit the same policy gate + field redaction the kernel applies to getEntity — a version read can expose redacted/older field values, so each returned version must pass through the identical redaction pipeline, not the raw store rows. Implement redaction-on-version before exposing. Additive: no migration, no breaking change.
features.recommendation: Build in v2.0 — this is the seeded, load-bearing gap and the single clearest case of a legitimate flow being forced into /unsafe. Add PolicyEnforcedKernel.listVersions(entityId, opts?) and getVersion(entityId, version) that delegate to the canonical store and run results through the existing read-gate + redaction path; expose both on ClusterSDK with typed records and JSDoc examples. Mirror the same surfacing on CLI + MCP (cross-surface version-history gap) so the three surfaces stay at parity.

features.title: Pagination (cursor + limit) on SDK list / search results
features.thesis_fit: App developer + AI agent. An app building a browse UI, or an agent iterating a large corpus, currently has no cursor/limit on list/search — results come back unbounded. That is a memory and latency cliff at scale and undermines 'trustworthy retrieval' the moment a store grows past toy size. Bounded, resumable reads are table stakes for a federated DB that holds artifacts and a growing ledger.
features.scope: src/sdk/cluster-sdk.ts (list/search signatures), src/kernel/policy-enforced-kernel.ts (thread limit/cursor through the gate), and the underlying index/canonical store list/search contracts. Return a typed page envelope { items, nextCursor }.
features.dependencies: Likely a list/search contract signature change (return a page envelope instead of a bare array, accept {limit,cursor}) → set breaking. Pairs naturally with FEAT-SDK-006 (filtering) — design the query options object once. No data migration.
features.recommendation: Build in v2.0 (2.0.0 is already MAJOR, so the return-shape break is acceptable — call it out in the changelog). Introduce a stable cursor (opaque, content/offset-based, not leaking internal IDs) and a default page size. Gate + redaction must apply per page, not per full set.

features.title: Typed receipt + lineage reads on ClusterSDK (read provenance without /unsafe)
features.thesis_fit: AI agent + operator. The provenance/receipt spine is the product's differentiator — content-addressable provenance + integrity-checked receipts. The lifecycle the brief names ends in 'read receipt', and operators need 'why does this entity exist / what produced it / show me the lineage'. If the SDK lacks typed getReceipt(id) / walkLineage(entityId) and these live only on the ledger store contract, the auditing agent is pushed into /unsafe to do the one thing the system is built to make trustworthy.
features.scope: src/sdk/cluster-sdk.ts (typed getReceipt / getLineage), src/kernel/policy-enforced-kernel.ts (policed ledger reads, if not already exposed). Confirm against the ledger store contract.
features.dependencies: Depends on confirming whether any policed ledger read path is already exposed on the kernel (inspection was blocked — see notes). If a raw ledger read exists, reuse its gate; otherwise add one. Additive. NOTE: this is read-side provenance only — it is NOT the externally-anchored/signed tamper-proof ledger upgrade, which is explicitly out of scope.
features.recommendation: Build in v2.0 if not already present. Return typed Receipt and Lineage records (integrity status surfaced as a typed field, not a raw hash blob) with JSDoc that walks ingest→...→commit→receipt. VERIFY FIRST whether ClusterSDK already exposes these — if it does, downgrade/drop; if it only exists on /unsafe, this is a real gap.

features.title: Batch ingest / batch commit with per-item receipts and partial-failure semantics
features.thesis_fit: AI agent. An agent ingesting hundreds of artifacts or committing a wave of proposed mutations currently pays per-call gate + receipt + round-trip overhead N times, with no atomic-ish grouping. Bulk is the dominant agent write pattern; one-at-a-time is the throughput bottleneck of the safe mutation lifecycle.
features.scope: src/sdk/cluster-sdk.ts (ingestMany / commitMany), src/kernel/policy-enforced-kernel.ts (batched gate evaluation while preserving one receipt per item + per-item provenance).
features.dependencies: Hard design dependency: partial-failure semantics. The gate must be evaluated per item (a batch cannot smuggle one item past policy), each item still needs its own integrity-checked receipt, and the result must report per-item success/failure rather than all-or-nothing. Best built AFTER FEAT-SDK-001/002 so the typed result/envelope conventions already exist.
features.recommendation: Defer to v2.x unless agent bulk-ingest is a near-term named workload. Additive, but large and easy to get wrong (receipt integrity + partial failure). When built, return a typed BatchResult[] with per-item receipt or typed error — never collapse failures into a single throw.

features.title: Field-level filtering / query predicates from the SDK (reduce over-fetch and /unsafe pressure)
features.thesis_fit: AI agent + app developer. If the SDK only offers coarse get-by-id + free-text search, any 'canonical entities where field = X / status = Y' need forces the caller to over-fetch then filter client-side — wasteful at scale — or drop into /unsafe to hit the store directly. Expressive, gated filtering keeps efficient retrieval inside the safe surface.
features.scope: src/sdk/cluster-sdk.ts (query options), src/kernel/policy-enforced-kernel.ts (push predicates down through the gate so filtering never sees redacted-away fields), index/canonical store contracts.
features.dependencies: Should share the query options object with FEAT-SDK-002 (pagination) — design them together to avoid two competing query shapes. Redaction caveat: predicates on redacted fields must be rejected/empty, not silently evaluated against raw values.
features.recommendation: Defer to v2.x and co-design with pagination. Keep the predicate surface deliberately small (equality + a few operators on whitelisted fields); explicitly reject a full query language as bloat (see notes).

features.title: Generic / typed result records across the SDK (compile-time safety for entity payloads)
features.thesis_fit: App developer. If ClusterSDK returns loose/unknown shapes, every consumer casts and loses compile-time safety — friction and bug surface for the integrator. A generic ClusterSDK<TSchema> (or per-call type params) lets app code get well-typed records straight from ingest/retrieve/version reads.
features.scope: src/sdk/cluster-sdk.ts type signatures, src/index.ts exported types.
features.dependencies: If existing method signatures change to generics, that is a breaking type change (acceptable in 2.0.0). LOW CONFIDENCE — I could not read cluster-sdk.ts (output suppressed, see notes); if the API is already strongly typed, downgrade to a JSDoc/examples polish item only.
features.recommendation: VERIFY current type strength first. If shapes are loose, add generics in v2.0 alongside the version-history/receipt types so the typed surface lands coherently. If already typed, drop this.

features.title: createSafeCluster() ergonomics — sane defaults + clean local↔Postgres selection + simpler principal/policy wiring
features.thesis_fit: App developer. The factory is the front door to a policed cluster. If standing one up requires hand-assembling principal + policy + capabilities + backend selection with thin defaults, first-integration friction is high and pushes people toward /unsafe to 'just get something working'. A one-liner safe default lowers the on-ramp without weakening the gate.
features.scope: src/index.ts (createSafeCluster signature + defaults), config types.
features.dependencies: LOW CONFIDENCE — could not read index.ts (output suppressed). Additive (defaults + overloads). If ergonomics are already clean, this collapses to a docs/quickstart item.
features.recommendation: VERIFY current factory shape. If wiring is heavy, add a defaulted overload (e.g. createSafeCluster({ backend: 'local' }) with a sensible default principal/policy and explicit, typed Postgres opts) in v2.0. Keep the secure-by-default posture — never default to an over-permissive policy.

features.title: Close /unsafe coverage gaps — guarantee no legitimate flow requires the escape hatch (verify-or-retract the package-boundary claim)
features.thesis_fit: App developer + AI agent. The whole point of the /safe vs /unsafe split is that the safe policed handle covers real work and /unsafe is a rare, advertised escape. Every legitimate use-case that is forced into /unsafe (version history today — FEAT-SDK-001; likely lineage/receipt reads — FEAT-SDK-009) is a hole in the safe surface AND, if docs/package-boundary.md asserts the safe surface is sufficient, a documentation promise the code does not keep.
features.claim_location: docs/package-boundary.md — line TBD (could not read the file: tool output was suppressed this session; the synthesis lead must locate the specific 'safe surface covers X / use /unsafe only for Y' assertion and confirm/retract).
features.scope: docs/package-boundary.md, docs/sdk.md, the export surface in src/index.ts vs src/unsafe.ts.
features.dependencies: Resolved largely by shipping FEAT-SDK-001 and FEAT-SDK-009 (the two flows currently most likely to force /unsafe). After those land, re-audit the boundary doc.
features.recommendation: Treat as implement-or-retract: after the version-history + lineage/receipt reads are surfaced, either update package-boundary.md to reflect the now-complete safe surface, OR explicitly document the remaining intentional /unsafe-only operations. Do NOT ship 2.0 with a 'safe surface is complete' claim while version history is /unsafe-only. (claim_location line unverified — see notes.)

features.title: Streaming reads for large artifacts and long-running ops (verify/doctor) from the SDK
features.thesis_fit: App developer + operator. The artifact store holds raw immutable files/outputs that can be large; retrieving them is all-or-nothing today, with memory pressure and no progress signal. Long ops (verify/doctor over a big cluster) likewise return only at completion. Streaming/iterables would make large-artifact retrieval and long diagnostics usable in real apps.
features.scope: src/sdk/cluster-sdk.ts (return AsyncIterable / stream for artifact reads and long ops), kernel + artifact store contract.
features.dependencies: Needs streaming support threaded through the artifact store contract and the gate (gate must authorize before the stream opens; integrity check semantics on a streamed artifact need defining). Build after pagination establishes the iteration conventions.
features.recommendation: Defer to v2.x. This is the weakest item on the list — real pain (large artifacts) but a large architectural lift; keep it as a tracked follow-up, not a 2.0 commitment. Reassess once a concrete large-artifact workload is named.
