CROSS-CUTTING: (1) Version history is not just an SDK gap — it is a cross-surface gap (kernel + SDK + CLI + MCP). FEAT-SDK-001 should be implemented at the kernel layer once and surfaced on all three surfaces for parity; coordinate with the CLI/MCP domains. (2) Redaction-on-read is the recurring dependency: version reads, paginated reads, filtered reads, and lineage reads must ALL pass through the same redaction pipeline as getEntity — never return raw store rows. This is the single most important correctness constraint across this domain's features. (3) Pagination (002) + filtering (006) share a query-options shape — design them together to avoid two competing query APIs. (4) The 'no legitimate flow needs /unsafe' guarantee (008) is largely DISCHARGED by shipping 001 + 009; sequence them first, then re-audit the boundary doc.

REJECTED AS BLOAT (discipline trail): (a) A full query/DSL or GraphQL-style language on the SDK — over-engineered for an AI-native KV/contract store; a small whitelisted-predicate filter (006) covers the real pain. (b) Change-feeds / subscriptions / reactive watch() on the SDK — no named user pain surfaced for 2.0; the ledger already records mutations, so polling lineage suffices for now. (c) ORM-style entity classes / active-record wrappers — generics (004) give type safety without the weight. (d) Anything touching the externally-anchored / cryptographically-signed tamper-proof ledger — explicitly out of scope per the brief (tracked v2.x). (e) I declined to invent additional speculative items beyond the brief's enumerated candidates precisely because I could not read the code to confirm a real gap — padding the list with unverifiable findings would violate THE BAR.

domain: D4 — Operator surface (CLI) and lifecycle ops

features.title: Map diagnostic failures to non-zero exit codes (verify / doctor / health)
features.claim_location: docs/cli.md:57 ("Exit codes: 0 on success, 1 on any failure"); reinforced docs/cli.md:14-16
features.thesis_fit: Operator: the thesis promises 'real diagnostics'. A diagnostic that reports corruption but exits 0 is not a real diagnostic — it silently passes CI/cron and the operator never learns the cluster is unsound. Directly serves the operator half of the one-line thesis.
features.scope: src/cli.ts handleVerify (316-337), handleDoctor (341-362), handleHealth (434-461). Set process.exitCode=1 when result.ok===false / status!=='ok'.
features.dependencies: none — the result objects already carry ok/status; the CLI just fails to act on them. Breaking because scripts currently relying on exit 0 from a failing verify will start failing (correctly).
features.recommendation: v2.0. Three-line fix per handler: `if (!result.ok) process.exitCode = 1;`. This is the single highest-leverage operator fix in the domain — it makes `db-cluster verify && deploy` actually safe. Confirmed in code: none of the three diagnostic handlers set exitCode. operations.md:58 already tells operators to 'alert on non-zero exit OR ok:false' — the 'OR ok:false' is a workaround the doc author added precisely because the exit code is broken. Retract that workaround once fixed.

features.title: Implement --version / -v (currently documented, falls through to help)
features.claim_location: docs/cli.md:60-62 ("Run db-cluster --version to print the CLI version")
features.thesis_fit: Operator/app-dev: version is the first thing anyone files in a bug report or pins in automation. A documented flag that prints help instead erodes trust in the whole CLI surface. Cheap correctness debt on the operator front door.
features.scope: src/cli.ts parseArgs/main switch; read version from package.json. cli.ts:447-449 explicitly documents the gap.
features.dependencies: none.
features.recommendation: v2.0. Add `--version`/`-v` handling that prints package.json version as plain text (and a `{ "version": "x" }` object under --json). Implement-not-retract: every CLI ships --version; retracting would be the wrong call.

features.title: TTY-aware color + NO_COLOR / FORCE_COLOR support
features.claim_location: docs/cli.md:68 ("Color output is auto-disabled when stdout is not a TTY")
features.thesis_fit: Operator/app-dev: piping `db-cluster get id > file` today embeds raw ANSI escapes (unless --json), corrupting logs and downstream parsers. The doc promises auto-disable; the code only disables on explicit flags. Scripting ergonomics.
features.scope: src/cli/color-output.ts (no isTTY/NO_COLOR/FORCE_COLOR anywhere — confirmed by grep) + cli.ts:79 where createColorOutput is called.
features.dependencies: none.
features.recommendation: v2.0. Gate color on `process.stdout.isTTY && !NO_COLOR`, honor `FORCE_COLOR` for CI logs (no-color.org de-facto standard). Implement-not-retract — the documented behavior is the correct behavior. (Noted but out of scope: printHelp calls color.join('\n') which returns '' — `db-cluster help` prints almost nothing; latent bug in color-output.ts:37-42, flag to bug-pass not feature-pass.)

features.title: Progress feedback for long-running ops (--progress / streaming counters)
features.thesis_fit: Operator: 'operators get real diagnostics'. A multi-minute rebuild/verify/backup that prints nothing is indistinguishable from a hang — the operator can't tell a slow verify from a deadlocked one, and on a 100k-entry store (rebuild.ts:41) that's minutes of blind waiting. Removes the core lifecycle-ops friction at scale.
features.scope: src/ops/rebuild.ts, verify.ts, backup.ts, restore.ts, migrations.ts (all annotated V2-C1-009) + cli.ts handlers. Root cause at SDK layer: sdk/index.ts:58-60 — exportAll/reindex return fully-materialized arrays, no progress events.
features.dependencies: Two-tier. CHEAP TIER (no SDK change): rebuild already loops entry-by-entry (rebuild.ts:30-36), so the ops/CLI layer can emit a `N/M` counter to stderr under a --progress flag for rebuild today. FULL TIER: verify/backup/exportAll need streaming SDK variants or a progress callback param — depends on an SDK signature change.
features.recommendation: v2.0 ship the cheap tier (rebuild + migrate stderr counters gated on --progress, TTY-aware so JSON/pipes stay clean). Defer streaming verify/backup to v2.x behind the SDK streaming work (pairs with FEAT-CLI-009 resumability). This is the seeded V2-C1-009 residual — pull the visible win into 2.0, defer the SDK-deep part.

features.title: signals command — drain the OperatorSignal channel to the operator
features.thesis_fit: Operator: the kernel ALREADY emits structured signals on integrity mismatch, policy denial, store degradation, receipt anomaly, canonical conflict (operator-signal.ts:24-31) into a working ring buffer — but no surface drains them, so every one of those safety-relevant events is invisible. This is the operator's window into the security spine the v1.x hardening built. Closing it makes the hardening observable.
features.scope: src/kernel/operator-signal.ts (RingBufferSignalSink.drain()/peek() already implemented). Needs: SDK accessor (drainSignals/peekSignals — absent per sdk/index.ts:69-70) + new `db-cluster signals [--drain] [--json]` command in cli.ts. operations.md:34-36 documents it as 'planned but not yet available'.
features.dependencies: SDK accessor must be added first (small — the ring buffer + drain/peek already exist in the kernel; the SDK just doesn't expose them). Then a thin CLI command. No engine work; the emit side is already wired.
features.recommendation: v2.0. This is the seeded KERNEL-C-012 item and it is worth pulling in BECAUSE the hard part (emit plumbing + bounded buffer) is already shipped — only the surface is missing. Add `drainSignals(): OperatorSignal[]` / `peekSignals()` to the SDK, then `db-cluster signals` (peek by default, --drain to clear, --json for scripting, --since/--severity filters as polish). Retract the operations.md 'planned' note once landed. Lower-risk than it looks; do NOT defer to v2.x.

features.title: versions command — inspect canonical version history / lineage from the CLI
features.thesis_fit: Operator + AI agent: the thesis sells 'trustworthy retrieval + provenance/lineage'. Versioning exists in the engine (canonical/versioning.ts; GetResult.version is populated, sdk/index.ts:55-57) but an operator triaging a bad write cannot answer 'what did this entity look like before, and which mutation changed it' without writing SDK code. orphan-mutations.md:26-27 explicitly flags 'no ledger inspect / version-history command' as a tracked gap.
features.scope: src/canonical/versioning.ts (engine has it) → needs SDK accessor (listVersions/getVersion — explicitly absent, sdk/index.ts:63) → new `db-cluster versions <id> [--at <n>] [--json]` in cli.ts.
features.dependencies: Requires an SDK accessor first (listVersions(id), getVersion(id, n)). This is the CLI facet of the cross-surface version-history gap — coordinate with the SDK/MCP domains so one SDK method serves all three surfaces. Not purely a surface gap.
features.recommendation: v2.0 IF the shared SDK accessor lands (it should — it's the same dependency the SDK and MCP facets need). The CLI wrapper is then small. Ship `versions <id>` (list) + `versions <id> --at <n>` (fetch historical) + --json. This is the seeded version-history CLI facet.

features.title: Local metrics surface — counters + latency on health (no egress)
features.thesis_fit: Operator: health.ts:46-49 calls this 'the single biggest observability gap'. Today `health` answers 'up?' but never 'how well?' — no read/write/denial counters, no latency, no uptime, no entity/mutation/receipt counts. An operator triaging a degraded cluster is flying blind. In-process, pull-based, zero egress — squarely inside the product's stated observability boundary.
features.scope: src/ops/health.ts (point-in-time only, no metrics) + a counters source in the kernel. Surface as `db-cluster health --metrics --json` and/or `db-cluster stats`. Policy denials already flow through the kernel (and emit OperatorSignals), so denial counts are reachable.
features.dependencies: Needs lightweight in-kernel counters (reads, writes, mutations, denials, errors) — modest engine instrumentation. No schema migration. Synergizes with FEAT-CLI-005 (signals already surfaces denial events; this aggregates them).
features.recommendation: v2.0 for cluster-wide COUNTS (entities/mutations/receipts/denials) via a `stats` command — cheap, high operator value, mostly aggregation over existing stores. Defer latency histograms + OpenMetrics text exposition (health.ts:51-53) to v2.x — useful but heavier, and not load-bearing for the 2.0 thesis. Strictly local; do not conflate with egress telemetry (explicitly out of bounds).

features.title: Structured error envelope (code/hint) + differentiated exit codes for CI
features.thesis_fit: Operator/app-dev automating the CLI: the --json error envelope is `{ error: "free text" }` with NO code field (errors.ts:25-28), so a script cannot branch on error TYPE — only presence. Exit is binary 0/1 (errors.ts:30-32) so CI can't react differently to config-error vs integrity-failure vs store-unavailable. OPS_ERRORS codes are defined but unused by the CLI. Scripting ergonomics.
features.scope: src/ops/errors.ts (OPS_ERRORS exists, OpsError has code+hint) + cli.ts fail() (line ~390-397, emits free text, no code). Wire fail() to emit { error: { code, message, hint } } under --json and a small exit-code map.
features.dependencies: none in code. Breaking because the --json error shape changes ({error: string} → {error: {code,message,hint}}) and exit codes diversify beyond 0/1 — both observable contract changes, appropriate for the major.
features.recommendation: v2.0 (it's a major — take the breaking shape change now). Adopt the existing OPS_ERRORS codes in fail(), populate hint, and define a documented exit-code scheme (e.g. 2=usage, 3=config, 4=store-unavailable, 5=integrity). Document the catalog in docs/cli.md (errors.ts:37 notes no catalog exists). Pairs naturally with FEAT-CLI-001.

features.title: Resumable / checkpointed rebuild + restore
features.thesis_fit: Operator: rebuild is not resumable — interrupted at entry 50k of 100k, the next run starts from scratch (rebuild.ts:44-45). On a large cluster a transient interruption (deploy, OOM, Ctrl-C) wastes the entire multi-minute run. Graceful degradation under partial failure for the primary recovery path.
features.scope: src/ops/rebuild.ts (no checkpoint) and backup.ts/restore.ts (full-only, no resume). Needs a checkpoint cursor persisted between runs.
features.dependencies: Depends on the SDK streaming/cursor work (sdk/index.ts:58-60 — exportAll/reindex are whole-array, no cursor). Same SDK dependency as the full tier of FEAT-CLI-004; build them together.
features.recommendation: Defer to v2.x. Real value but large, and gated on the SDK streaming refactor. Not load-bearing for 2.0 — the progress counter (FEAT-CLI-004 cheap tier) addresses the acute 'is it hung?' pain; resumability is the follow-on. Bundle with streaming verify/backup.

features.title: doctor --fix for safe auto-remediation
features.thesis_fit: Operator: the index-stale runbook (index-stale.md) flow is diagnose→manually run rebuild→manually re-run verify. doctor detects a stale index but takes no action (doctor.ts:28-30, 56-57). A guarded --fix that runs the obviously-correct remediation (rebuild a detected-stale index) collapses three manual steps into one. Removes routine-ops friction.
features.scope: src/ops/doctor.ts + cli.ts handleDoctor. Limit --fix to provably-safe, non-destructive remediations (index rebuild); never auto-touch ledger/canonical.
features.dependencies: Builds on rebuild (exists). Should require explicit --fix (never default) and ideally a confirmation/--yes. Safer once FEAT-CLI-004 progress exists (a --fix that triggers a silent multi-minute rebuild is its own footgun).
features.recommendation: Defer to v2.x. Genuinely useful but a convenience layer, not a thesis gap — the runbook already documents the manual path and it works. Scope must stay strictly to safe derived-store rebuilds; explicitly out of scope for anything touching the append-only ledger.

features.title: Confirmation guard (--yes) on destructive lifecycle ops
features.thesis_fit: Operator: migrate/restore/rebuild mutate all stores with no confirmation prompt or --yes flag (migrations.ts:33-35). migrate is forward-only with no rollback (migrations.ts:27-28), so a mistaken `db-cluster migrate` against prod has restore-from-backup as the only recovery. A fat-finger guard on irreversible ops is standard operator hygiene.
features.scope: src/cli.ts handleMigrations/handleRestore/handleRebuild. Prompt when interactive (TTY) unless --yes; always proceed under --yes/non-TTY for automation.
features.dependencies: Cleanest once TTY detection exists (FEAT-CLI-003) so prompting only happens interactively and never breaks pipes/CI.
features.recommendation: Defer to v2.x (or fold into 2.0 if FEAT-CLI-003 lands, since it shares the isTTY plumbing). Keep it non-blocking for automation — guard only the interactive path. Low priority because operators of a v1 data product are typically deliberate, but the asymmetry (irreversible migrate, backup-only recovery) makes it cheap insurance.

notes:
CODEBASE STATE: The CLI and ops modules are unusually well-annotated — the author left explicit tracked-gap notes inline (cli.ts:447-479, every ops/*.ts trailer, sdk/index.ts:49-70). My findings corroborate those notes against the actual code paths and the docs; I treated the notes as leads, not as ground truth, and verified each (e.g. confirmed via grep that no isTTY/NO_COLOR exists and that no version/policy/export/config/signals/reconcile command cases exist in cli.ts).

ACTUAL CLI COMMAND SET (cli.ts switch): put, get, search, list, mutate, verify, doctor, rebuild, backup, restore, health, migrate, help. That's it.

THE THREE SEEDED ITEMS, all addressed: (1) V2-C1-009 progress → FEAT-CLI-004 (split into a cheap stderr-counter tier shippable in 2.0 and an SDK-streaming tier deferred). (2) KERNEL-C-012 OperatorSignal → FEAT-CLI-005 (recommend PULLING INTO 2.0 — the ring buffer + drain/peek are already implemented and the kernel already emits; only the SDK accessor + CLI command are missing, so the risk is low and the payoff is making the v1.x security spine observable). (3) version-history CLI facet → FEAT-CLI-006 (gated on a shared SDK accessor that the SDK and MCP facets need too — coordinate cross-domain).

CROSS-CUTTING ROOT CAUSE: several CLI gaps are actually SDK-surface gaps. sdk/index.ts exposes no listVersions, no drainSignals, no streaming/cursor variants, no AbortSignal, no batch ops, no dispose(). The CLI cannot expose what the SDK does not surface. The synthesis lead should treat 'SDK accessor for versions' and 'SDK accessor for signals' as shared dependencies feeding multiple domains, not as CLI-only work. Recommend one SDK PR that adds listVersions/getVersion + drainSignals/peekSignals, unblocking FEAT-CLI-005, FEAT-CLI-006, and their MCP/SDK twins at once.

HIGHEST-LEVERAGE SINGLE FIX: FEAT-CLI-001 (exit codes on verify/doctor/health). It is three lines, it is a documented-contract violation, and it is an active CI/cron hazard — a failing integrity verify currently exits 0. Ship it in 2.0 regardless of what else makes the cut.

CLAIM-MISMATCH CONCENTRATION: docs/cli.md is the offender (lines 14-16, 57, 60-62, 68). docs/operations.md is by contrast honest (it candidly documents V2-C1-009, the signals 'planned' status, and backup limitations) — leave it; just retract its line-58 'OR ok:false' workaround once exit codes are fixed. README makes no CLI exit-code/--version claims (grep clean), so the implement-or-retract decisions are localized to docs/cli.md.

REJECTED AS BLOAT (discipline trail): (a) Backup compression / incremental-differential backup (backup.ts:55,63) — framed in the code as a scale concern but I could not attach a NAMED user with concrete near-term pain for a v1 product; pure scale-speculation. Defer until a real operator hits backup-size pain. (b) health --watch terminal dashboard (health.ts:43) — a nicety; `watch db-cluster health --json` from the shell covers it, no thesis fit. (c) OpenMetrics/Prometheus text exposition (health.ts:51) — folded as the deferrable tail of FEAT-CLI-007 rather than its own feature; real but not 2.0-load-bearing. (d) rebuild targets beyond 'index' / search-ranking-stats rebuild (rebuild.ts:47) — speculative, no second derived store exists to rebuild yet. (e) `db-cluster reconcile` for orphan mutations (orphan-mutations.md:22-24) — deliberately NOT proposed: reconciliation writes compensating ledger entries, which is a correctness-sensitive mutation-lifecycle design question that overlaps the ledger domain and the out-of-scope tamper-proof-ledger track; flagging it here for the synthesis lead to route to the ledger/SDK domain rather than claiming it as a CLI feature.

OUT-OF-BOUNDS RESPECTED: did not audit or propose the externally-anchored/signed tamper-proof ledger upgrade. Noted only that the append-only ledger (orphan-mutations.md:15) is the substrate it would build on.

domain: D5 — Storage, scale, and observability (adapters, dashboard, store-contracts, Postgres/TLS trust model). ID prefix FEAT-STORE.

features.title: Postgres parity: artifact / index / ledger stores (full federated cluster on Postgres)
features.thesis_fit: Operator: the thesis sells a 'production-grade' federated cluster, but today only the canonical store is durable/concurrent on Postgres — artifact, index, and ledger silently fall back to single-node file adapters even when backend=postgres (src/adapters/factory.ts:50-57). The ledger is the provenance/receipt backbone; running it on a non-atomic local file under a concurrent app means the trust spine is the LEAST scalable store. Removes the friction of 'I configured Postgres for durability but my event log still corrupts under parallel writers.'
features.scope: src/adapters/postgres/ (add postgres-artifact-store.ts, postgres-index-store.ts, postgres-ledger-store.ts), src/adapters/postgres/schema.ts + new migrations 003/004/005, src/adapters/factory.ts (wire them), src/adapters/index.ts (export them). Artifact content storage decision: bytea vs large-object vs filesystem-pointer.
features.dependencies: Needs new SQL migrations and a migration-runner (FEAT-STORE-007) to be usable without hand-applied DDL. Ledger store on Postgres should use a real sequence/serial for monotonic seq instead of array.length+1. Independent of other features otherwise.
features.recommendation: Build for v2.0 — this is the headline parity feature that makes 'Postgres backend' honest. Sequence the ledger store first (most write-heavy, most thesis-critical), then index, then artifact. For artifact content, recommend bytea for small/medium blobs with a documented size ceiling, and keep the door open to an S3 pointer adapter (FEAT-STORE-010) for large blobs rather than bloating Postgres. Honestly disclosed today (README:35-36, architecture.md:33-34) so it is not a claim-mismatch — but shipping 2.0 'with the v2.0 feature set' while the cluster is still canonical-only on Postgres undercuts the whole 'federated cluster' positioning.

features.title: Real viewer dashboard app (or retract the README 'React dashboard demo' claim)
features.claim_location: README.md:79 (Dashboard section, lines 77-83): "A viewer-only React dashboard demo lives in dashboard/. It reads through the SDK and renders counts, an entity inspector, and the ledger feed."
features.thesis_fit: Operator/app-dev: the README promises a runnable React viewer that renders counts, an inspector, and the ledger feed. No such app exists — there are NO .tsx/.jsx files anywhere in the repo; dashboard/ contains a single apply-redaction.d.ts whose own header (lines 4-5) states 'The .tsx components referenced in the README are not present in the repo.' The view-MODELS exist (src/dashboard/dashboard-model.ts, inspector-data.ts, ops-model.ts) but there is no UI to consume them. An operator who reads the README and looks for the dashboard finds nothing to run.
features.scope: dashboard/ (currently only apply-redaction.d.ts), src/dashboard/*.ts (view-models — already built), README.md:77-83.
features.dependencies: View-models already exist and the SDK can feed them, but the SDK does not currently expose buildDashboardModel/buildOpsModel/buildInspectorData (see FEAT-STORE-009) — a real dashboard needs that surface or must import internal modules.
features.recommendation: Implement-or-retract decision. Cheapest honest path for v2.0: RETRACT — rewrite the README section to say 'view-models for a viewer dashboard live in src/dashboard/; a reference UI is not bundled,' and delete the orphan dashboard/apply-redaction.d.ts. If a UI is wanted as a product differentiator, build a thin single-file static viewer (counts + inspector + ledger feed) wired to the view-models and SDK — but that is a v2.x polish item, not a 2.0 blocker. Do NOT ship 2.0 with the current false 'React dashboard demo lives in dashboard/' text.

features.title: Driver-managed Postgres TLS as a real, opt-in implemented feature
features.thesis_fit: Operator: TLS was retracted in the hardening pass (SECURITY.md:31-33) because the prior implementation gave a false sense of security; today db-cluster passes the connection string to pg as-is and the operator must hand-roll sslmode=require, a TLS proxy, or a private network (SECURITY.md:23-29, README:'bring your own TLS'). The docs are honest, so this is NOT a current claim-mismatch — it is a missing capability. The friction: a safe-by-default product makes the operator responsible for the single most important wire-security control, and a forgotten sslmode silently ships plaintext credentials. Thesis is 'operators get safe writes' — safe writes over an unencrypted DB link is a hole.
features.scope: src/contracts/config.ts (add a typed ssl/tls option — e.g. ssl: boolean | { ca, rejectUnauthorized, ... }), src/adapters/factory.ts:49 (pass ssl into the Pool config, not just connectionString), src/util/config-loader.ts (env + file plumbing, e.g. DB_CLUSTER_PG_SSL), SECURITY.md (update the trust-model section once real).
features.dependencies: Independent. Should land alongside FEAT-STORE-008 (pool config) since both extend the same Pool construction and config schema — do them in one config-surface pass.
features.recommendation: Build for v2.0, but learn from why it was pulled: do NOT silently default TLS on/off in a way that masks reality. Make it explicit and typed — accept a structured ssl option (CA bundle, rejectUnauthorized), validate it, and have `db-cluster doctor` report the resolved TLS mode of the live connection so the operator can SEE whether the link is encrypted. The prior impl failed by being implicit; the fix is explicitness + an observable surface, not just re-adding a flag.

features.title: Append-friendly local ledger (stop O(n) full-file rewrite per event)
features.thesis_fit: AI agent + operator: the ledger is the provenance/receipt/lineage spine and the MOST write-heavy store (every mutation appends an event — see kernel put/delete paths). LocalLedgerStore loads the entire events.json into memory (local-ledger-store.ts:18,28) and re-serializes + rewrites the WHOLE array on every append (lines 36,51-52). That is O(n) memory and O(n) per append → O(n^2) to build a log, plus a non-atomic full-file write that architecture.md:39 admits 'are not atomic under parallel writers.' A long-running agent that records thousands of receipts degrades and risks a torn ledger on crash — directly undermining 'trustworthy provenance.'
features.scope: src/adapters/local/local-ledger-store.ts (append path, persistence format), and the same pattern in src/adapters/local/local-index-store.ts:34. Consider NDJSON append (one event per line, O(1) append) or a sqlite-backed ledger (see FEAT-STORE-006).
features.dependencies: If solved via sqlite, folds into FEAT-STORE-006. If solved via NDJSON, independent but changes the on-disk format (migration of existing .cluster/ledger/events.json — provide a one-time converter). seq generation must stay monotonic without reading the whole file (track head in a sidecar or use line count lazily).
features.recommendation: Build for v2.0 if a real workload is expected; the ledger is the worst offender and the most thesis-critical. Cheapest fix: NDJSON append for the local ledger (O(1) append, crash-tolerant per-line, tail-readable). Cleaner long-term fix: route the local ledger through sqlite (FEAT-STORE-006). Recommend NDJSON now if avoiding a new dependency, sqlite if FEAT-STORE-006 is greenlit.

features.title: Streaming artifact I/O for large blobs (read/write without full in-memory buffering)
features.thesis_fit: App-dev/AI agent: the artifact store is for 'raw immutable files, text, model outputs' and store-contracts.md:23 explicitly claims 'Large blobs supported.' But LocalArtifactStore.getContent reads the entire file into a Buffer (local-artifact-store.ts:38) and put() takes a fully-materialized Buffer|string and writes it in one shot (lines 43-48, contract artifact-store.ts:12). A multi-hundred-MB model output (a realistic AI artifact) is loaded entirely into Node heap on every read/write — OOM risk and latency spike. 'Large blobs supported' is aspirational given the implementation.
features.scope: src/contracts/artifact-store.ts (add streaming variants: putStream(Readable, mediaType), getContentStream(id) -> Readable), src/adapters/local/local-artifact-store.ts (createReadStream/createWriteStream + streaming sha256 via crypto.Hash transform), kernel wrapArtifact (src/kernel/policy-enforced-kernel.ts:73-96) to gate + receipt the streaming paths.
features.dependencies: Content-hash-as-id (content-addressable design) requires hashing the stream as it is written — straightforward with a hash transform, but the id is only known after the stream completes, so the write-to-temp-then-rename pattern is needed for atomicity. Pairs naturally with an S3 artifact adapter (FEAT-STORE-010).
features.recommendation: Add streaming as an additive contract method for v2.0 (keep buffer-based put/get for small artifacts). Either implement true streaming or soften the 'Large blobs supported' line in store-contracts.md:23 to name a practical size ceiling. Recommend implementing streaming — 'model outputs' in the thesis implies large artifacts are a primary use-case, not an edge case.

features.title: SQLite local adapter (concurrent, atomic alternative to the file adapter)
features.thesis_fit: App-dev/operator: there is a hard gap between the two current backends — local (single-node, non-atomic file rewrites, O(n) index/ledger) and Postgres (needs a server, canonical-only). architecture.md:39 admits the local adapter is 'not designed for high write concurrency.' A single-file sqlite adapter closes that gap: ACID transactions, real indexes, FTS5 for the index store, monotonic rowids for the ledger, WAL-mode concurrency — all with zero server to run. It is the natural 'serious single-node' tier and would give ALL FOUR stores parity in one adapter (unlike the partial Postgres path).
features.scope: New src/adapters/sqlite/ (four stores + schema), src/contracts/config.ts (backend enum add 'sqlite' + sqlitePath), src/adapters/factory.ts (wire it). FTS5 virtual table satisfies the index store's 'pluggable richer backend' promise (store-contracts.md:35-36).
features.dependencies: Adds a native dependency (better-sqlite3 or node:sqlite if targeting Node 22+ — note devDeps already target @types/node ^22). Overlaps FEAT-STORE-004 (sqlite ledger fixes the append problem for free) and FEAT-STORE-011 (FTS5 gives real search). Decide sqlite-vs-Postgres-parity priority: sqlite gives full four-store parity faster than completing Postgres parity.
features.recommendation: Strong candidate but scope-heavy — pick ONE of {complete Postgres parity (FEAT-STORE-001), sqlite adapter} as the v2.0 storage headline, not both. Recommend evaluating sqlite FIRST: it delivers full federated parity + atomic ledger + FTS5 search in a single adapter with no server, which arguably serves the 'embed a trustworthy cluster' thesis better than a partial Postgres. If the user base is server-deployed, prioritize Postgres parity instead. Flag for an explicit product decision.

features.title: Postgres migration runner (stop requiring hand-applied SQL)
features.thesis_fit: Operator: schema.ts:6-10 states bluntly 'There is no migration runner; operators apply SQL by hand: psql $DATABASE_URL -f migrations/001_initial.sql ...'. An operator must manually run each migration in order, track which ran, and re-do it on every environment — error-prone, and a forgotten migration (e.g. 002_canonical_versions) silently breaks listVersions/getVersion at runtime. There is no docker-compose, no .env example, no `db-cluster migrate` command. This is real first-run friction for the 'operators get safe writes + real diagnostics' half of the thesis.
features.scope: New `db-cluster migrate` CLI subcommand (src/cli/index.ts — note the CLI help comment at :11-12 already advertises subcommands that aren't implemented), a schema_migrations bookkeeping table, a runner that applies pending files from src/adapters/postgres/migrations/ in order. Optionally a docker-compose.yml + .env.example for a turnkey local Postgres.
features.dependencies: Becomes MORE important once FEAT-STORE-001 adds artifact/index/ledger Postgres tables (more migrations to manage). Build the runner before or with parity, not after.
features.recommendation: Build for v2.0 alongside Postgres parity — parity adds 3+ new tables, and 'apply six SQL files by hand in the right order' is not a shippable operator story for a 'production-grade' backend. A 50-line runner + a schema_migrations table removes the friction. Ship a docker-compose.yml too so an operator can stand up Postgres + run migrations in two commands.

features.title: Postgres connection resilience: pool config, timeouts, retry/reconnect
features.thesis_fit: Operator: the Postgres pool is constructed with ONLY { connectionString } (factory.ts:49) — no max pool size, no idleTimeoutMillis, no connectionTimeoutMillis, no statement timeout, and the config schema (config.ts) has nowhere to set them. There is no retry/reconnect on a dropped connection; a transient DB blip surfaces as a raw error to the caller, and there is no pool-error handler (an unhandled pool 'error' event can crash the process). For 'durable, concurrent, production-grade' (README:33), an operator cannot tune or harden the connection at all.
features.scope: src/contracts/config.ts (add optional pool block: max, idleTimeoutMillis, connectionTimeoutMillis, statement_timeout), src/adapters/factory.ts:44-57 (thread into Pool, attach pool.on('error') handler), optional bounded retry wrapper around transient query failures in postgres-canonical-store.ts.
features.dependencies: Shares the config-surface + Pool-construction change with FEAT-STORE-003 (TLS) — do both in one pass. Retry semantics must respect the existing transaction in put() (FOR UPDATE / BEGIN — retrying mid-transaction needs care; retry at the operation boundary, not inside an open tx).
features.recommendation: Build for v2.0 with FEAT-STORE-003 as a single 'Postgres connection hardening' change. At minimum: expose pool sizing + timeouts and attach a pool error handler (prevents process-crash on idle-connection errors). Retry/reconnect is a nice-to-have; the pg Pool already reconnects lazily, so prioritize the error handler + tunables over a custom retry loop.

features.title: Expose dashboard / ops / inspector view-models on the public SDK
features.thesis_fit: App-dev: the SDK (ClusterSDK, src/sdk/index.ts) exposes only health() (liveness). The genuinely useful observability view-models — buildDashboardModel (counts), buildOpsModel (counts+health), buildInspectorData (entity + related ledger events) — exist in src/dashboard/ but are NOT reachable from the SDK handle. An app developer who wants counts or an entity inspector must import internal src/dashboard/* modules (not part of the package's exports map — package.json only exports '.' and './mcp'), coupling their code to internals. The CLI and MCP can call these; the SDK consumer is left out.
features.scope: src/sdk/index.ts (add cluster.stats() -> DashboardModel, cluster.ops() -> OpsModel, cluster.inspect(id) -> InspectorData delegating to the existing builders), docs/SDK.md.
features.dependencies: None — the builders already exist and take ClusterStores; the SDK already holds the (policy-wrapped) stores. Trivial delegation. A real bundled dashboard (FEAT-STORE-002) would consume these SDK methods.
features.recommendation: Build for v2.0 — small, high-leverage symmetry fix. The view-models are already written and used by CLI/MCP; not surfacing them on the SDK is an oversight, not a design choice. One caveat: decide whether SDK stats()/inspect() go through the policy-enforced facade (they should, for the confidentiality guarantees the hardening pass established) — currently the CLI builders run on RAW stores (cli/index.ts:20,26,32 call buildStores directly, bypassing the kernel), so align the SDK path to the enforced stores.

features.title: S3 / object-store artifact adapter for large or high-volume artifacts
features.thesis_fit: Operator/app-dev: the artifact store holds 'raw immutable files, text, model outputs' (often large, often many). The only durable backend is Postgres-canonical (no artifact support at all yet), so durable artifact storage means cramming blobs into a DB or relying on the local filesystem. An S3/object-store adapter is the industry-standard home for large immutable content-addressed blobs (the id IS the content hash — a perfect S3 key), offloading bytes from the DB and the local disk and giving real durability + scale for the artifact tier.
features.scope: New src/adapters/s3/s3-artifact-store.ts implementing ArtifactStore (get/getContent/put/list/delete), config.ts (artifact-backend selection + bucket/endpoint/credentials), factory.ts (per-store backend wiring — see note below).
features.dependencies: Requires PER-STORE backend selection (today config picks ONE backend for the whole cluster; an S3 artifact store + Postgres canonical + sqlite index is a mix the current ClusterConfig cannot express — config.ts only has a single `backend` enum). That config refactor is the real prerequisite and is itself a breaking-ish schema change. Pairs with streaming (FEAT-STORE-005). Adds an AWS SDK dependency.
features.recommendation: Defer to v2.x. Only build once (a) Postgres/sqlite parity (FEAT-STORE-001/006) proves the per-store adapter wiring, and (b) there is a named user with large-artifact volume. The honest blocker is per-store backend config, not the S3 code itself — surface that as the gating refactor. Do NOT build speculatively; the local adapter is adequate for dev/single-node artifact use today.

features.title: Pluggable / richer index backend (full-text or vector), as the contract already promises
features.claim_location: docs/store-contracts.md:35-36: "Naive substring search today; pluggable for richer backends (full-text, vector) later."
features.thesis_fit: AI agent: the thesis is 'AI agents get trustworthy RETRIEVAL.' The index store is the discoverability surface, but it is a case-insensitive substring scan over an in-memory array (local-index-store.ts:48-58) with NO pluggability seam — IndexStore is a fixed contract and the factory hardcodes LocalIndexStore. The docs promise 'pluggable for richer backends (full-text, vector) later,' but there is no abstraction that makes a full-text or vector backend droppable today. For an AI agent doing semantic retrieval, substring matching is the weakest possible search and won't find paraphrased/semantic matches.
features.scope: src/contracts/index-store.ts (the contract is already the seam — the gap is implementations + config to select them), a sqlite FTS5 index store (folds into FEAT-STORE-006) for full-text, and optionally a vector index store. docs/store-contracts.md:35-36 (align claim to reality).
features.dependencies: Full-text comes nearly free with the sqlite adapter (FTS5) — strongly couple this to FEAT-STORE-006. Vector search adds an embedding dependency and is a much larger lift (out of scope for a storage pass — it touches retrieval semantics, not just storage).
features.recommendation: Partial implement-or-retract. For v2.0: if sqlite (FEAT-STORE-006) ships, get FTS5 full-text essentially for free and the 'full-text later' half of the claim becomes true. Vector search should be explicitly deferred to v2.x (or the word 'vector' softened in store-contracts.md:36 to avoid an open-ended promise). Recommend: ship FTS5 with sqlite, retract/defer the vector mention until there is a real plan.

notes:
EVIDENCE SUMMARY (all confirmed by reading, READ-ONLY pass): (1) Postgres parity gap CONFIRMED — src/adapters/postgres/ contains only postgres-canonical-store.ts + schema.ts + migrations 001/002; index.ts:7 exports only PostgresCanonicalStore; factory.ts:50-57 wires Local artifact/index/ledger even when backend=postgres (explicit inline comment 'artifact/index/ledger still use local adapters even in postgres mode'). This is honestly disclosed in README:35-36 and architecture.md:33-34/41-42, so it is missing-feature, NOT claim-mismatch. (2) NO React dashboard CONFIRMED — zero .tsx/.jsx files in the entire repo (find returned empty); dashboard/ holds one file, apply-redaction.d.ts, whose own header (lines 4-5) states the .tsx components 'are not present in the repo.' README:77-83 describes a runnable demo → genuine claim-mismatch (FEAT-STORE-002). (3) TLS CONFIRMED retracted and honestly documented — SECURITY.md:21-33; no ssl anywhere in config.ts/factory.ts (grep empty). Framed as missing-feature per the seed (FEAT-STORE-003), not a current claim-mismatch.

CROSS-CUTTING OBSERVATIONS: (a) The local file adapters (index, ledger) share an anti-pattern: load-entire-collection-into-memory + rewrite-whole-file-on-every-write (local-index-store.ts:16/34, local-ledger-store.ts:18/36). The ledger is the worst case because it is append-heavy and is the thesis trust-spine. (b) Observability EXISTS but is shallow: clusterHealth is ping-only liveness; dashboard-model exposes COUNTS only (no bytes/sizes/last-rotation/disk-usage); CLI has health/stats/doctor/ops but output is JSON-only with no human-readable mode. I did NOT raise a separate 'add metrics' feature because counts+liveness are already surfaced on CLI+MCP — the real observability gaps are folded into FEAT-STORE-009 (SDK exposure) and the size/volume metrics noted here. (c) NOTED-NOT-AUDITED latent issues (out of scope — foundation is solid per instructions, and these are correctness not features): ops/health.ts:39 returns { ok, checkedAt } and omits the built `stores` array (health detail is computed then dropped); several files have a stray trailing `}` after the final export (config.ts, store.ts, receipt.ts, kernel, cli, factory.ts has a line-number oddity at 40/50) and local stores use require('node:fs') inside ESM (local-artifact-store.ts:72-73, local-canonical-store.ts:72). Flagging only as context; not proposing fixes. (d) CLI help comment (cli/index.ts:11-12) advertises subcommands get/put/search/ledger/verify that are not implemented in the switch — a CLI/UX-domain item, left to that auditor. (e) The CLI ops builders run on RAW stores via buildStores() (cli/index.ts:20/26/32), bypassing the PolicyEnforcedKernel — relevant to the surface-confidentiality work the hardening pass did; noted inside FEAT-STORE-009's recommendation.

REJECTED AS BLOAT (discipline): — Generic 'add Prometheus/OpenTelemetry metrics' — rejected: egress/telemetry is explicitly out of bounds, and local counts+liveness already exist; the gap is depth (sizes/rotation) not a metrics framework. — 'Add caching layer to the index/canonical stores' — rejected: no named user pain at current scale, and it would add invalidation complexity over a foundation that should first get its adapters right (sqlite/Postgres parity solve the real perf problem properly). — 'Multi-region / replication / sharding for Postgres' — rejected: speculative, no named user, and Postgres itself handles replication operationally; out of scope for a library. — 'Backup/restore/export-import CLI for the local adapter' — considered, rejected for now: the local store is plain files an operator can copy; a dedicated tool has weak pain until sqlite (single opaque file) lands, at which point a backup command becomes worth it (would attach to FEAT-STORE-006, not stand alone). — The externally-anchored/cryptographically-signed tamper-proof ledger upgrade — explicitly OUT OF SCOPE per instructions; noted only that the receipt/ledger spine exists (provenance/receipt.ts, local-ledger-store.ts) and would be the attach point. PRIORITY RATIONALE: FEAT-STORE-001 is the only CRITICAL — it is the difference between 'federated cluster' being true or marketing. FEAT-STORE-002/003/004 are HIGH (false README claim; credential-plaintext risk; trust-spine perf). The user must make ONE explicit call: sqlite adapter (FEAT-STORE-006, full four-store parity in one shot) vs completing Postgres parity (FEAT-STORE-001) as the v2.0 storage headline — building both is scope-bloat for one release.
